title: Bluesky leans into AI with Attie, an app for building custom feeds
summary:
Bluesky has introduced a new AI application named Attie, designed to empower users to create personalized content feeds without requiring coding skills. This innovative app was unveiled at the Atmosphere conference by Jay Graber, the former CEO of Bluesky, and current Chief Innovation Officer, alongside CTO Paul Frazee. Attie leverages Anthropic's Claude AI technology and operates on Bluesky's AT Protocol, allowing users to interact with the app using natural language commands. This marks a significant shift from Bluesky's traditional social networking focus, as Attie is a standalone product aimed at enhancing user engagement through customization.

The app allows users to curate their feeds by simply typing commands, making the process intuitive and accessible. Users can ask Attie for content recommendations based on their preferences, and the app will adapt to their interests by analyzing data shared across the Bluesky ecosystem. This feature not only enhances user experience but also encourages a more dynamic interaction with content, as users can shape their feeds in real-time without needing technical expertise.

Interim CEO Toni Schneider emphasized that Attie represents a new frontier for Bluesky, enabling a broader audience to engage with the platform's capabilities. By facilitating the creation of custom feeds, Bluesky aims to foster a more personalized and user-driven environment. As the app enters beta testing with conference attendees, it is poised to redefine how users interact with social media, potentially setting a new standard for content curation in the digital space.
social_text: Bluesky's new AI app Attie allows users to create custom feeds using natural language commands, leveraging Anthropic's Claude AI. This standalone product aims to enhance user engagement and personalization in the Bluesky ecosystem.

title: Stock market today: Dow, S&P 500, Nasdaq futures fall entering shortened week ahead of jobs report
summary:
U.S. stock futures experienced a decline as Wall Street entered a shortened trading week ahead of significant economic data releases. The Dow Jones Industrial Average futures fell by 0.5%, while S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 futures dropped by 0.4%. This downturn follows a challenging week where all three major indexes closed with substantial losses, with the Dow plunging nearly 800 points and the S&P 500 reaching its lowest level in months. This marks the fifth consecutive weekly decline for the S&P 500, pushing the Dow and Nasdaq into correction territory, reflecting deteriorating market sentiment amidst ongoing geopolitical tensions, particularly the U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran, which has now extended into its second month.

The recent market instability has been exacerbated by the performance of the so-called 'Magnificent Seven' tech companies, which have collectively lost $850 billion in market value over the past week. Notably, Meta and Google faced a significant setback after losing a landmark lawsuit regarding their responsibilities in managing social media addiction. This has raised concerns among investors about the future profitability and regulatory landscape for these tech giants, which were previously seen as resilient amid economic uncertainties.

As the market looks ahead, investors are particularly focused on upcoming labor market indicators, including the Job Openings and Labor Turnover Survey (JOLTS) and the ADP private payrolls report. The March jobs report is anticipated to be crucial, especially following the volatility in job numbers observed in January and February. Additionally, earnings reports from companies like Nike may provide insights into consumer behavior, while USA Rare Earth and Trilogy Metals will shed light on the minerals sector's health, further influencing market sentiment in the coming days.
social_text: U.S. stock futures fell as Dow, S&P 500, and Nasdaq 100 slid ahead of key labor market data. The Dow dropped nearly 800 points last week, pushing it into correction territory amid geopolitical tensions and tech losses totaling $850 billion.

title: ASX Small Ordinaries to slip as companies progress financing, resources and project milestones
summary:
The S&P/ASX Small Ordinaries Index (ASX: XSO) experienced a decline of 0.87%, closing at 3,315.40, although it remains up 3.56% over the past week. This fluctuation reflects the ongoing adjustments in the small-cap sector as companies navigate financing and project milestones. Despite the recent dip, several firms are making significant strides in their respective projects, indicating a resilient outlook for the small-cap market. 

Cobalt Blue Holdings Ltd has made notable progress by entering a consortium agreement with Glomar Minerals LLC to develop a critical minerals processing facility in the United States. This partnership aims to conduct feasibility studies for processing polymetallic nodules, with pilot tests set to take place at Cobalt Blue’s Broken Hill Technology Centre. The facility is being positioned as a potential world-first plant, targeting the supply of processed materials to the US advanced manufacturing and defense sectors, which could have substantial implications for the industry.

In addition, Alkane Resources Ltd has secured A$150 million in new financing through a syndicated debt package, which includes a A$110 million revolving credit facility and a A$40 million contingent instrument facility. This funding will enhance the company's liquidity and support general corporate purposes, following the early repayment of a previous finance facility. Furthermore, the Supreme Court of British Columbia has approved a deal between Tamboran Resources Corporation and Falcon Oil & Gas, marking another significant development in the small-cap sector as companies continue to advance their strategic initiatives amidst market fluctuations.
social_text: The ASX Small Ordinaries Index fell 0.87% to 3,315.40, despite a 3.56% weekly gain. Cobalt Blue partners with Glomar for a US processing facility, while Alkane secures A$150M in new financing for growth.

title: US Futures Drop, Oil Rises as Iran Conflict Widens: Markets Wrap
summary:
US equity futures experienced a decline while oil prices surged as tensions escalated in the Middle East, particularly with the involvement of Iran-backed Houthi militants in the ongoing conflict. This development has raised concerns among investors about the potential for a prolonged war, which could keep oil prices elevated. Brent crude saw an increase of up to 3.4% in early trading, reflecting the market's reaction to the heightened geopolitical risks. Meanwhile, S&P 500 contracts fell by 0.5%, indicating a bearish sentiment among traders, with Asian stock futures also expected to open lower.

The situation intensified over the weekend as Israel conducted airstrikes on Tehran and Saudi Arabia intercepted multiple drones, further complicating the regional dynamics. The deployment of an additional 3,500 US troops to the Middle East underscores the seriousness of the situation and the potential for further escalation. Analysts, such as Matt Maley from Miller Tabak + Co., suggest that this escalation may lead to sustained high oil prices, which could negatively impact market performance.

In the wake of these developments, risk assets have begun to show signs of weakness, with the S&P 500 experiencing its worst two-day decline in a year, dropping 3.6%. This decline has left the index 8.8% below its January peak. Additionally, the Nasdaq 100 fell into a 10% correction after a 4.3% drop over the same period. Concerns about inflation have also led to losses in government bonds, with yields rising and expectations for Federal Reserve interest rate cuts diminishing. The cryptocurrency market is feeling the pressure as Bitcoin fell to its lowest level in over three weeks, reflecting a defensive stance among traders amid the market volatility.
social_text: US equity futures dropped 0.5% and Brent crude rose 3.4% as Iran-backed Houthi militants escalated conflict. 3,500 US troops deployed, raising fears of prolonged war and high oil prices. S&P 500 fell 3.6% over two days.

title: EV battery startup pivots to defense industry amid Iran war, weak electric vehicle market
summary:
Sion Power, an Arizona-based battery startup, is shifting its focus from electric vehicle (EV) batteries to the aerospace and defense sectors due to the ongoing war in Iran and increasing demand for U.S. drones. Led by CEO Pamela Fletcher, a former General Motors executive, the company plans to commercialize its high-energy lithium-metal battery cells for defense applications later this year. This pivot reflects a broader trend among companies that initially invested in EV technology but are now seeking opportunities in alternative markets as the automotive sector faces challenges. The slowdown in EV adoption and changes in government incentives have prompted many firms to explore other avenues for their battery production capacity.
